title: Flex Duct Installation
scenario: A technician installed 8 feet of flex duct from a trunk line to a supply boot in a residential attic. The flex is fully extended (not compressed) and supported every 5 feet.
explanation: Flex duct maximum length is 5 feet per SMACNA and most local codes. At 8 feet, this fails. Additionally, supports are required every 4 feet maximum, not 5. This install needs to be replaced with rigid duct for the first 3+ feet, then a maximum 5-foot flex connection to the boot.

title: Return Air Pathway
scenario: A residential HVAC system uses the space between floor joists as a return air pathway (panning). The joist bays are sealed with sheet metal panning on the bottom, and the space connects to the return air plenum at the furnace.
explanation: Building cavities (joist bays, stud spaces) can be used as return air pathways in residential construction when properly sealed with sheet metal panning. This is a common and code-compliant practice in residential systems. However, supply ducts cannot use building cavities — only return air.

title: Thermostat Location
scenario: A thermostat is installed on an interior wall, 5 feet above the floor, 3 feet from a supply register, and 2 feet from a window on an exterior wall. The thermostat is in direct sunlight from the window for about 2 hours per afternoon.
explanation: While the height and distance from the register are acceptable, direct sunlight on a thermostat causes false high temperature readings, making the AC run excessively. Thermostats must be installed away from direct sunlight, heat sources, supply registers (min 3' is borderline), and exterior walls. The sunlight exposure is the primary violation here.

title: Condensate Pump Installation
scenario: A mini-split indoor unit is installed in a basement with no floor drain nearby. The installer connected a condensate pump to the drain pan, with the pump discharge running 15 feet horizontally to a laundry sink. The pump has an overflow safety switch wired to shut down the unit.
explanation: This is a proper installation. When gravity drainage is not possible, condensate pumps are an accepted solution. The key requirement is the overflow safety switch — if the pump fails, the unit must shut down to prevent water damage. Discharge to a laundry sink is an approved indirect waste receptor.

title: Gas Furnace Clearance
scenario: A gas furnace is installed in a utility closet with 1 inch of clearance on each side and 3 inches above the top. The manufacturer's installation manual specifies minimum 1 inch side clearance and 2 inches top clearance.
explanation: The installation meets the manufacturer's minimum clearances. Equipment clearances are determined by the manufacturer's listing and installation instructions, which are enforced by code. As long as the actual clearances meet or exceed the listed minimums, the installation is compliant.

title: Outside Air Damper
scenario: A commercial rooftop unit has a motorized outside air damper that is set to provide 15% outside air at all times during occupied hours. The building is an office with 20 occupants in the zone served by this unit.
explanation: ASHRAE 62.1 requires minimum outside air based on occupancy (typically 5 CFM/person + 0.06 CFM/sq ft for offices). A flat 15% may be too much or too little depending on the unit size and space. The code requires calculation-based ventilation, not arbitrary percentages. A 5-ton unit at 15% OA might only deliver 300 CFM — which could be under the required minimum for 20 occupants.

title: Duct Material in Garage
scenario: Sheet metal supply and return ductwork runs through an attached residential garage to serve bedrooms above. All duct joints are sealed with mastic and the duct is insulated with foil-faced insulation.
explanation: Sheet metal duct is allowed in garages when properly sealed. The key requirement is that ductwork in garages must not have openings (registers/grilles) in the garage space to prevent carbon monoxide from vehicle exhaust from entering the duct system. Sealed sheet metal running through the garage to serve other spaces is acceptable.

title: Refrigerant Detector
scenario: A mechanical room houses a 50-ton chiller using R-410A refrigerant. The room has a refrigerant leak detector mounted at 12 inches above the floor, connected to an alarm and exhaust fan. The detector is calibrated annually.
explanation: This is correct. ASHRAE 15 and IMC require refrigerant leak detection in mechanical rooms when the refrigerant charge exceeds certain thresholds. R-410A is heavier than air, so the detector at 12" above floor is properly placed. The alarm and exhaust fan activation are required safety responses. Annual calibration meets the standard.

title: Dryer Exhaust Length
scenario: A residential clothes dryer has a 4-inch diameter smooth metal exhaust duct running 30 feet with two 90-degree elbows to the exterior wall termination. The total equivalent length is 40 feet (30' straight + 5' per elbow).
explanation: IRC limits dryer exhaust to 35 feet maximum developed length (including equivalent length for fittings). At 40 feet equivalent, this exceeds the maximum. Options: reroute for a shorter path, eliminate one elbow, or install a dryer exhaust booster fan listed for the application. Excessive length causes lint buildup — a leading cause of house fires.

title: Economizer Operation
scenario: A commercial AHU economizer is set to open the outside air damper to 100% when the outside air temperature drops below 55°F, effectively providing free cooling. The system has an enthalpy sensor and a minimum OA position of 20%.
explanation: Economizer operation using outdoor air for free cooling is not only allowed but required by energy codes for most commercial systems. Using enthalpy (temperature + humidity) control is the preferred method. The 55°F changeover and 100% OA capability are standard economizer settings. The 20% minimum OA ensures ventilation is always maintained.

title: CO Detector in Mechanical Room
scenario: A mechanical room with a gas-fired boiler has a carbon monoxide detector installed at breathing height (5 feet) on the wall near the entrance. The detector is hardwired with battery backup and connected to the building alarm system.
explanation: CO detectors are required in mechanical rooms with fuel-burning equipment. Mounting at breathing height (5 feet) is correct for CO since it mixes with air (similar density). Hardwired with battery backup and connection to the building alarm are best practices that meet or exceed code requirements.

title: Smoke Damper Bypass
scenario: During a renovation, an HVAC contractor discovered a smoke damper in a duct penetrating a smoke barrier. The damper's actuator is broken and parts are on a 6-week backorder. The contractor wired the damper open and left it in the open position to maintain airflow to the occupied space.
explanation: Smoke dampers are life-safety devices and cannot be bypassed or disabled under any circumstances without AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) approval and a formal fire watch plan. The contractor should have notified the building owner, fire marshal, and obtained a variance. Wiring a smoke damper open without authorization is a serious code violation that puts occupants at risk.

title: Union Training: Kitchen Hood Makeup Air
scenario: A commercial kitchen exhaust hood is installed with 2,000 CFM exhaust capacity. The makeup air unit provides only 1,200 CFM (60% of exhaust). The kitchen has negative pressure that makes exterior doors difficult to open and causes drafting issues at the gas cooking equipment.
explanation: IMC 508.1 requires that makeup air be provided at a rate approximately equal to the exhaust air. The accepted minimum is 80% of the exhaust airflow. At 1,200 CFM makeup against 2,000 CFM exhaust (60%), this installation is significantly under the requirement. The 800 CFM deficit creates negative pressure that makes doors hard to open, disrupts gas appliance draft (CO risk), and pulls unconditioned air through every crack in the building envelope. A proper install needs at least 1,600 CFM (80%) makeup air. This is a common scenario taught in union training because makeup air is one of the most frequently undersized components in kitchen ventilation.

title: Union Training: Smoke Damper in Duct Penetration
scenario: A SMACNA apprentice installs a combination fire/smoke damper where a supply duct penetrates a 2-hour fire-rated wall. The damper is a UL listed assembly with a properly sized sleeve, fire caulk at the wall penetration, and the smoke damper actuator is interlocked to the building fire alarm system for automatic closure on alarm.
explanation: This is a textbook-correct installation as taught in SMACNA apprenticeship programs. IMC 607 requires fire dampers at duct penetrations through fire-rated walls, and NFPA 90A requires smoke dampers at smoke barrier penetrations. Using a combination fire/smoke damper satisfies both requirements. The key elements are all present: UL listed assembly (matches the wall rating), proper sleeve (maintains the wall's fire rating), fire caulk (seals the annular space), and interlock to the fire alarm (ensures the smoke damper closes when smoke is detected). Union halls emphasize this installation because fire/smoke damper work is a high-liability specialty that commands premium wages.

title: Duct Insulation Flame Spread Rating
scenario: A mechanical contractor installs fiberglass duct insulation on supply ductwork in a commercial office building. The insulation product has a flame spread index of 20 and a smoke developed index of 45, both tested per ASTM E84. The insulation is applied to ductwork running through a ceiling plenum return air space.
explanation: This installation is compliant. NFPA 90A requires that duct insulation materials have a flame spread index of 25 or less and a smoke developed index of 50 or less when tested per ASTM E84 (Standard Test Method for Surface Burning Characteristics of Building Materials). At 20 flame spread and 45 smoke developed, this insulation meets both thresholds. These limits are especially critical in plenum spaces where fire and smoke can spread rapidly through the return air path.

title: Flexible Duct Through Fire Wall
scenario: During a tenant improvement project, a sheet metal worker runs a 10-inch flexible duct through a 1-hour fire-rated wall to connect a new VAV box to an existing trunk duct on the other side. The flex duct is 4 feet long and is supported on both sides of the wall penetration. A fire damper is installed at the wall penetration.
explanation: Flexible duct is prohibited from penetrating any fire-rated wall, floor, or ceiling assembly regardless of whether a fire damper is installed. NFPA 90A strictly limits flexible duct use — it cannot pass through walls, floors, or ceilings. Additionally, flexible duct is limited to a maximum length of 5 feet total. The correct approach is to use rigid sheet metal duct through the wall penetration with a properly installed fire damper, then transition to flex duct (if needed) on the room side only.

title: Acoustic Duct Liner Velocity
scenario: An engineer specifies acoustic duct liner for a supply duct serving a conference room to reduce fan noise. The liner has an NRC rating of 0.75 tested per ASTM C423. The duct is sized for 5,500 ft/min air velocity at design conditions. The liner is applied to the interior of the duct and secured with mechanical fasteners and adhesive.
codeReference: ASTM C423 / SMACNA HVAC Duct Construction Standards
explanation: This installation meets all requirements. The acoustic duct liner NRC (Noise Reduction Coefficient) of 0.75 exceeds the minimum 0.70 requirement per ASTM C423. The air velocity of 5,500 ft/min is below the maximum 6,000 ft/min limit for lined ductwork. Exceeding 6,000 ft/min can cause the liner to erode and release fibers into the airstream. Both mechanical fasteners and adhesive are required for proper liner attachment per SMACNA standards.

title: Elastomeric Duct Wrap K-Value
scenario: A mechanical insulator applies elastomeric rubber duct wrap to chilled water ductwork in a humid climate. The product submittal shows a thermal conductivity (K-value) of 0.32 Btu·in/hr·ft²·°F at 75°F, tested per ASTM C518. The specification requires a K-value of 0.25 or less at 75°F.
explanation: The elastomeric duct wrap fails the specification. The required K-value is 0.25 Btu·in/hr·ft²·°F at 75°F per ASTM C518, but the submitted product has a K-value of 0.32 — meaning it conducts 28% more heat than allowed. On chilled water ductwork in humid climates, inadequate insulation leads to condensation on the duct exterior, which causes mold growth, ceiling damage, and wasted cooling energy. The contractor must submit a product that meets the 0.25 K-value requirement.

title: Plenum-Rated Cable and Wire
scenario: During an HVAC renovation, a contractor runs standard PVC-jacketed thermostat wire through a ceiling plenum space to connect a new rooftop unit to the building automation system. The wire has a flame spread index of 60 and a smoke developed index of 120. The plenum space is used as a return air path.
explanation: All materials installed in plenum spaces used for return air must have a flame spread index of 25 or less and a smoke developed index of 50 or less per NFPA 90A. This standard PVC wire at 60 flame spread and 120 smoke developed grossly exceeds both limits. In a fire, the PVC jacket would produce dense toxic smoke that gets distributed throughout the building via the return air path. Plenum-rated (CMP) wire or conduit must be used in any space that handles return air.

title: Chilled Water Pipe Insulation Thickness
scenario: An insulator wraps a 4-inch chilled water supply pipe (42°F) inside a mechanical room with 1.0 inch of elastomeric insulation. The pipe is located indoors in an unconditioned space. Per California T-24 Table 120.3-A, cooling piping 40-60°F in the 4-6 inch range requires 1.0 inch of insulation.
explanation: This installation meets code. Per California T-24 Table 120.3-A, space cooling piping at 40-60°F in the 4-6 inch pipe size range requires 1.0 inch of insulation for interior installations. The 42°F chilled water falls within the 40-60°F range, and the 4-inch pipe is in the 4-6 inch column. If this pipe were routed outdoors, an additional 0.5 inch minimum would be required (1.5 inches total) per the exterior piping rule.

title: Kitchen Supply Duct Liner
scenario: A sheet metal contractor installs acoustic duct liner inside a supply duct that serves a commercial kitchen. The liner is fiberglass with an NRC rating of 0.80 and is secured with mechanical fasteners and adhesive. The contractor's reasoning is that the kitchen needs noise reduction from the high-velocity supply air.
explanation: Duct liner is NOT allowed inside kitchen supply ducts, regardless of NRC rating or installation quality. Grease-laden air can saturate fiberglass liner, creating a fire hazard and breeding ground for bacteria. The University spec explicitly prohibits internal duct liner on kitchen supply ductwork. Noise reduction in kitchen duct systems must be achieved through external duct wrap, sound attenuators, or duct silencers mounted upstream of the kitchen branch.

title: Exterior Duct Insulation R-Value
scenario: A mechanical contractor insulates an outdoor supply duct running between two buildings with R-8 duct wrap (DW-B type) and an aluminum jacket. The project is located in Climate Zone 3. The University spec requires R-8 minimum for supply ducts outside the building in climate zones 1-4, with R-12 required in zones 5-8.
explanation: This installation meets code. In Climate Zone 3, the minimum R-value for supply and return ducts located outside the building is R-8. The contractor correctly used DW-B insulation with a metal jacket, which is required for exterior duct applications to protect the insulation from weather, UV damage, and physical abuse. If this project were in Climate Zone 5 or higher, R-12 would be required instead of R-8.

title: Heating Water Pipe Insulation — Exterior Run
scenario: A 2-inch heating water pipe at 180°F runs 30 feet along the exterior of a building before re-entering through a wall penetration. The pipe is insulated with 1.5 inches of mineral wool (PI-A) with an aluminum jacket. Per T-24 Table 120.3-A, a 1.5-3 inch pipe at 141-200°F requires 2.0 inches indoors.
explanation: The insulation is too thin for an exterior application. While 2.0 inches is the base requirement for a 1.5-3 inch pipe at 141-200°F per T-24 Table 120.3-A, the exterior piping rule requires an additional 0.5 inch minimum. The correct thickness is 2.5 inches for this exterior run. Even the indoor base requirement of 2.0 inches was not met — the installer used only 1.5 inches, which is the value for pipes under 1.25 inches. Always check both the pipe size column AND the exterior adder when insulating outdoor piping.

title: University AHU Supply Air Temperature
scenario: On the University Student Housing project, the commissioning agent measures supply air from AHU-B1-1-1 (AAON H3-CRB-3, 2,800 CFM) at 57°F leaving the cooling coil. The schedule shows entering air at 80°F DB / 67°F WB and leaving air at 56.7°F DB / 55.7°F WB.
explanation: At 57°F, the measured supply air temperature is within acceptable tolerance of the scheduled 56.7°F LAT. ASHRAE commissioning guidelines allow +/- 2°F from scheduled values for air temperatures. The 80/67 entering condition and 56.7/55.7 leaving condition indicate the coil is performing a 23.3°F dry-bulb temperature drop while also dehumidifying the air (the wet-bulb drops from 67°F to 55.7°F). A 57°F reading during commissioning confirms the coil is properly sized and the chilled water flow is correct.

title: University Ceiling Exhaust Fan CFM Mismatch
scenario: A ceiling-mounted exhaust fan in a restroom at the University Student Housing project is measured at 75 CFM during TAB. The fan is tagged EF-100 (Greenheck G-097-VG, downblast centrifugal). The equipment schedule shows EF-100 at 100 CFM and 0.606" ESP.
explanation: The fan is underperforming by 25%. At 75 CFM, the EF-100 is delivering only 75% of its scheduled 100 CFM airflow. This could indicate a blocked duct, damper not fully open, or excessive static pressure in the ductwork. IMC Table 403.3 requires specific exhaust rates for restrooms (typically 50 CFM per water closet or 1 CFM/sq ft). While 75 CFM might still meet minimum code for a small restroom, the fan must be balanced to its scheduled 100 CFM. TAB tolerance per AABC is +/- 10%, meaning acceptable range is 90-110 CFM.

title: University AWHP Leaving Water Temperature
scenario: The air-to-water heat pump AWHP-1 (JCI Quantech QTH10035PJP) on the University Student Housing project is producing 130°F leaving water temperature. The schedule specifies EWT 100°F and LWT 120°F with a capacity of 1,670 MBH at 34°F ambient.
explanation: The leaving water temperature of 130°F exceeds the scheduled 120°F LWT by 10°F. Air-to-water heat pumps are rated at specific entering and leaving conditions — running AWHP-1 at 130°F LWT instead of 120°F significantly reduces capacity and COP. The 20°F delta-T (100°F to 120°F) is the design condition at which the 1,670 MBH capacity was certified. At 130°F LWT, the heat pump must work against a higher condensing temperature, reducing efficiency by roughly 2-3% per degree above rated LWT. The setpoint must be corrected to 120°F.

title: University Undersized Exhaust Grille for 200 CFM
scenario: On the University Student Housing project, a 6"x6" exhaust grille (EA6X6) is installed to serve a room requiring 200 CFM exhaust. The diffuser schedule shows EA6X6-100 is rated for 100 CFM maximum.
explanation: The EA6X6 grille is rated for only 100 CFM per the University schedule. Installing it on a 200 CFM exhaust point doubles the design velocity through the neck, creating excessive noise (well above NC-35 criteria for occupied spaces) and pressure drop far exceeding the 0.15" WG maximum. The correct grille is EA8X8-200, which has an 8"x8" neck sized for 200 CFM. Undersized grilles also cause the exhaust fan to work harder, increasing energy consumption and reducing fan life.

title: University Duct Liner in Kitchen Supply Duct
scenario: On the University Student Housing project, the contractor installed fiberglass duct liner inside the supply air ductwork serving the residential kitchen area to reduce noise from the air handler.
codeReference: SMACNA HVAC Duct Construction Standards / IMC 603.5
explanation: Duct liner is not permitted inside kitchen supply ductwork per the University specifications. Fiberglass duct liner in kitchen areas can trap grease particles and food odors, creating a fire hazard and unsanitary condition. The porous surface of duct liner is impossible to clean once contaminated with cooking grease. Noise attenuation in kitchen supply ducts must be achieved through external duct wrap, silencers, or sound-attenuating duct transitions — not internal liner. IMC 603.5 requires duct materials to be suitable for the application and environment.

title: University Full Bathroom Exhaust at 50 CFM
scenario: On the University Student Housing project, a ceiling exhaust fan in a full bathroom (with shower) is providing 50 CFM. The contractor states this meets code minimum for bathroom ventilation.
explanation: 50 CFM is the exhaust rate for a half bath (toilet and sink only) per the University plans. A full bathroom with a shower requires 100 CFM to adequately remove moisture and prevent mold growth. The additional moisture from showering roughly doubles the ventilation requirement compared to a half bath. At only 50 CFM, the bathroom will have persistent humidity issues, leading to mold on grout, ceiling damage, and poor indoor air quality. The fan must be upsized or the duct run corrected to deliver 100 CFM.

title: University Louver Free Area for 1000 CFM
scenario: On the University Student Housing project, an exhaust louver with 2.0 sq ft of free area is installed to serve a 1,000 CFM exhaust system. The louver is a storm-resistant type with drainable blades.
explanation: This installation is correct. Per the University louver schedule, 1,000 CFM requires a minimum of 2.0 sq ft free area. At this combination, the face velocity is 500 FPM (1,000 CFM / 2.0 sq ft = 500 FPM), which is the maximum recommended velocity for storm-resistant louvers per AMCA 550. The drainable blade design allows rain that enters the louver to drain back outside rather than entering the duct system. The louver size, type, and free area all meet the specification requirements.

title: high-rise office Flex Duct Length and Clamp Type
scenario: On the high-rise office Level 31 tenant improvement, a sheet metal worker installs a 4-foot flex duct run from a branch takeoff to a linear diffuser. The flex duct connections are secured with stainless steel worm-drive clamps and UL-listed mastic at both ends. The flex is fully extended with no kinks.
codeReference: high-rise office Mechanical Specification / NFPA 90A Section 4.3.10
explanation: This installation is fully compliant with the high-rise office specification. The 4-foot run is under the 5-foot maximum, stainless steel worm-drive clamps meet the SS worm clamp requirement, and UL-listed mastic provides the required sealant. The high-rise office spec is explicit about clamp type — zip ties, plastic clamps, and duct tape are all prohibited. The fully extended flex duct ensures minimum pressure drop and maximum airflow delivery.

title: high-rise office Return Air Grille Velocity
scenario: On the high-rise commercial office, a return air grille in an open office area on Level 30 is measured at 380 FPM face velocity during TAB. The grille is a fixed-bar type in the ceiling return plenum.
codeReference: high-rise office Mechanical Specification / SMACNA HVAC Systems Testing, Adjusting and Balancing
explanation: The 380 FPM face velocity is within the high-rise office specification maximum of 400 FPM for return air grilles. At velocities above 400 FPM, return grilles generate audible noise that disrupts office workers — typically NC-35 or higher, which exceeds the NC-30 criterion for open office environments. The 380 FPM reading leaves only 20 FPM of margin, so the TAB contractor should note this in the report. If future rebalancing increases airflow, this grille could exceed the limit.

title: high-rise office CRAC Vibration Isolation Deflection
scenario: On the high-rise commercial office, a Liebert MM40C CRAC unit in the IDF room is installed on spring-neoprene isolation mounts with a measured static deflection of 1.5 inches. The specification requires minimum 1-inch spring-neoprene mounts.
codeReference: high-rise office Mechanical Specification / ASHRAE Handbook — HVAC Applications Ch. 48
explanation: While the high-rise office specification states minimum 1-inch spring-neoprene mounts, it also requires a minimum 2-inch static deflection for CRAC unit vibration isolation. The 1.5-inch deflection does not meet the 2-inch minimum. Static deflection determines isolation efficiency — at 1.5 inches, the mounts provide approximately 90% isolation efficiency, while 2 inches provides approximately 95%. In a high-rise office building where the IDF room is directly above occupied space, the additional 5% isolation is critical for controlling low-frequency compressor vibration that travels through the structure.

title: high-rise office Duct Hanger Load and Spacing
scenario: On the high-rise commercial office, a 24"x18" rectangular supply duct (3.0 sq ft cross section) is supported by trapeze hangers spaced at 8 feet on center. Each hanger is carrying approximately 48 lbs including the weight of duct, insulation, and turning vanes.
codeReference: high-rise office Mechanical Specification / SMACNA HVAC Duct Construction Standards
explanation: This installation meets both high-rise office requirements. The duct cross section is 3.0 sq ft, which is under the 4 sq ft threshold — so 8-foot OC hanger spacing is correct (ducts over 4 sq ft require 4-foot OC spacing). The 48 lb load per hanger is under the 50 lb maximum. However, the load is close to the limit. If additional accessories like smoke detectors, access doors, or heavier insulation are added later, the hangers could become overloaded. Best practice is to document the hanger load margin in the commissioning report.

title: high-rise office Transfer Fan Thermostat and Exhaust Fan Control Conflict
scenario: On Level 32 of the high-rise commercial office, a Greenheck CSP-A510-VG transfer fan is wired to a thermostat set at 80°F, and a Greenheck CSP-A1050-VG exhaust fan on the same floor is wired to a thermostat set at 78°F instead of a timeclock. The contractor argues that the thermostat provides better energy savings by only running the exhaust fan when the space is warm.
codeReference: high-rise office Mechanical Specification / ASHRAE 62.1 / IMC 403
explanation: The transfer fan thermostat at 80°F is correct per the high-rise office specification. However, the exhaust fan must be controlled by a timeclock, not a thermostat. ASHRAE 62.1 requires continuous exhaust ventilation during occupied hours to maintain indoor air quality — exhaust provides the negative pressure that drives outdoor air into the building through makeup air pathways. A thermostat-controlled exhaust fan would shut off whenever the space temperature drops below 78°F, eliminating ventilation airflow and allowing CO2, VOCs, and other contaminants to accumulate. The contractor's energy savings argument ignores the ventilation code requirement. The exhaust fan must run on a timeclock aligned with building occupancy hours.
